section.title: The Kings of the North and South
section.sectionRationale: The heart of the final vision is a strikingly detailed prophecy of the wars between the kingdoms that arose from Alexander's empire, the kings of the North and South, converging on a despicable king who desecrates the temple, halts the sacrifice, and persecutes those loyal to God. The wise instruct many and are refined through suffering. The chapter shows God's total command of history's smallest turns, and the certain end of every proud tyrant who exalts himself against Him.
section.thesis: God foretells the tangled wars of the North and South and the rise of a blasphemous king who desecrates the sanctuary and crushes the faithful, yet the vision insists that all runs to God's appointed time, that the wise who know their God will resist and instruct many, and that the self-exalting tyrant will come to his end with no one to help him.
section.frameNote: Four beats: Persia, Greece, and the breakup of Alexander's empire; the long wars of the kings of the South and North; the despicable king who defiles the covenant and the wise who resist; and the king who exalts himself above every god and meets his appointed end.

units.label: Persia, Greece, and Alexander's divided empire
units.note: a kingdom broken to the four winds

verses.text: Now then, I will tell you the truth: Three more kings will arise in Persia, and then a fourth, who will be far richer than all the others. By the power of his wealth, he will stir up everyone against the kingdom of Greece.
Then a mighty king will arise, who will rule with great authority and do as he pleases.

But as soon as he is established, his kingdom will be broken up and parceled out toward the four winds of heaven. It will not go to his descendants, nor will it have the authority with which he ruled, because his kingdom will be uprooted and given to others.

units.label: The long wars of South and North

verses.text: The king of the South will grow strong, but one of his commanders will grow even stronger and will rule his own kingdom with great authority.

After some years they will form an alliance, and the daughter of the king of the South will go to the king of the North to seal the agreement. But his daughter will not retain her position of power, nor will his strength endure. At that time she will be given up, along with her royal escort and her father and the one who supported her.
But one from her family line will rise up in his place, come against the army of the king of the North, and enter his fortress, fighting and prevailing.
He will take even their gods captive to Egypt, with their metal images and their precious vessels of silver and gold. For some years he will stay away from the king of the North,
who will invade the realm of the king of the South and then return to his own land.
But his sons will stir up strife and assemble a great army, which will advance forcefully, sweeping through like a flood, and will again carry the battle as far as his fortress.
In a rage, the king of the South will march out to fight the king of the North, who will raise a large army, but it will be delivered into the hand of his enemy.
When the army is carried off, the king of the South will be proud in heart and will cast down tens of thousands, but he will not triumph.
For the king of the North will raise another army, larger than the first, and after some years he will advance with a great army and many supplies.
In those times many will rise up against the king of the South. Violent ones among your own people will exalt themselves in fulfillment of the vision, but they will fail.
Then the king of the North will come, build up a siege ramp, and capture a fortified city. The forces of the South will not stand; even their best troops will not be able to resist.
The invader will do as he pleases, and no one will stand against him. He will establish himself in the Beautiful Land, with destruction in his hand.

He will resolve to come with the strength of his whole kingdom, and will reach an agreement with the king of the South. He will give him a daughter in marriage in order to overthrow the kingdom, but his plan will not succeed or help him.
Then he will turn his face to the coastlands and capture many of them. But a commander will put an end to his reproach and will turn it back upon him.
After this, he will turn back toward the fortresses of his own land, but he will stumble and fall and be no more.
In his place one will arise who will send out a tax collector for the glory of the kingdom; but within a few days he will be destroyed, though not in anger or in battle.

units.label: The despicable king and the faithful who resist

verses.text: In his place a despicable person will arise; royal honors will not be given to him, but he will come in a time of peace and seize the kingdom by intrigue.
Then a flood of forces will be swept away before him and destroyed, along with a prince of the covenant.
After an alliance is made with him, he will act deceitfully; for he will rise to power with only a few people.

In a time of peace, he will invade the richest provinces and do what his fathers and forefathers never did. He will lavish plunder, loot, and wealth on his followers, and he will plot against the strongholds, but only for a time.
And with a large army he will stir up his power and his courage against the king of the South, who will mobilize a very large and powerful army but will not withstand the plots devised against him.
Those who eat from his provisions will seek to destroy him; his army will be swept away, and many will fall slain.
And the two kings, with their hearts bent on evil, will speak lies at the same table, but to no avail, for still the end will come at the appointed time.
The king of the North will return to his land with great wealth, but his heart will be set against the holy {{berith|covenant}}; so he will do damage and return to his own land.
At the appointed time he will invade the South again, but this time will not be like the first.

Ships of Kittim will come against him, and he will lose heart. Then he will turn back and rage against the holy covenant and do damage. So he will return and show favor to those who forsake the holy covenant.
His forces will rise up and desecrate the temple fortress. They will abolish the daily sacrifice and set up the {{shiqquts|abomination of desolation}}.
With flattery he will corrupt those who violate the covenant, but the people who know their God will firmly resist him.
Those with {{maskilim|insight}} will instruct many, though for a time they will fall by sword or flame, or be captured or plundered.
Now when they fall, they will be granted a little help, but many will join them insincerely.
Some of the wise will fall so that they may be refined, purified, and made spotless until the time of the end, for it will still come at the {{moed|appointed time}}.

units.label: The king who exalts himself and his end
units.note: he will come to his end with no one to help

verses.text: Then the king will do as he pleases and will exalt and magnify himself above every god, and he will speak monstrous things against the God of gods. He will be successful until the time of wrath is completed, for what has been decreed must be accomplished.
He will show no regard for the gods of his fathers, nor for the one desired by women, nor for any other god, because he will magnify himself above them all.
And in their place, he will honor a god of fortresses, a god his fathers did not know, with gold, silver, precious stones, and riches.
He will attack the strongest fortresses with the help of a foreign god and will greatly honor those who acknowledge him, making them rulers over many and distributing the land for a price.

At the time of the end, the king of the South will engage him in battle, but the king of the North will storm out against him with chariots, horsemen, and many ships, invading many countries and sweeping through them like a flood.
He will also invade the Beautiful Land, and many countries will fall. But these will be delivered from his hand: Edom, Moab, and the leaders of the Ammonites.
He will extend his power over many countries, and not even the land of Egypt will escape.
He will gain control of the treasures of gold and silver and over all the riches of Egypt, and the Libyans and Cushites will also submit to him.
But news from the east and the north will alarm him, and he will go out with great fury to destroy many and devote them to destruction.
He will pitch his royal tents between the sea and the beautiful holy mountain, but he will meet his end with no one to help him.

berith.gloss: A covenant, the sacred bond between God and His people. The persecuting king sets his heart against the holy covenant, attacking not just a nation but the covenant relationship with God, and he rewards those who forsake it. The whole conflict is over loyalty to God's covenant.

shiqquts.gloss: The abomination of desolation, a detestable thing that defiles and lays waste. The king's forces halt the daily sacrifice and set up this abomination in the temple. Jesus takes up the very phrase in His teaching about coming judgment, showing the pattern reaches beyond its first fulfillment.

maskilim.gloss: Those who have insight, the wise. In the persecution they instruct many and stand firm, yet some fall and are refined through suffering. The word links to Daniel 12, where the wise shine like the stars; true wisdom is faithfulness to God that teaches others and endures, even to death.

moed.gloss: An appointed time, a set season. Again and again the vision insists that events, even the persecution and the tyrant's fall, will come at the moed, the time God has fixed. Nothing runs past God's schedule; the suffering is bounded, and the end arrives exactly when He has decreed.

basic.context: The angel gives Daniel an astonishingly detailed forecast of the centuries after him: the last Persian kings, the rise of Greece under a mighty king (Alexander), and the breakup of his empire into rival kingdoms, chiefly the king of the South (Egypt) and the king of the North (Syria). Their endless wars converge on a despicable king who defiles the temple, stops the sacrifice, sets up an abomination, and persecutes the faithful. The wise resist and teach others, some dying, and finally the tyrant exalts himself above every god and comes to his end.
basic.hermeneutics: This chapter reads like history written in advance, and its precision is why it matters: God knows and governs the smallest turns of politics and war. Interpreters largely agree the earlier verses trace the Greek and Egyptian-Syrian struggles up to Antiochus IV, who persecuted the Jews around 167 BC. Views differ on whether the closing verses stay with Antiochus or shift to a final antichrist. The steady message is that God rules history and every proud tyrant meets his appointed end.
basic.originalLanguages: The tyrant sets himself against the holy berith, the covenant, and sets up the shiqquts, the abomination that desolates. Against him stand the maskilim, the wise who know their God, instructing many and refined through suffering. And repeatedly the vision anchors everything to the moed, the appointed time God has fixed.
basic.typology: The desecration of the temple and the halting of sacrifice, with the abomination of desolation, form a pattern Jesus applies to later judgment, and which points to the ultimate defiler defeated by Christ. The wise who suffer, teach, and are refined foreshadow the faithful witnesses of every age who share in Christ's sufferings.
basic.christ: The self-exalting king who blasphemes the God of gods and defiles the sanctuary is the very type of the antichrist, whom Christ will destroy. Against him, the people who know their God stand firm, a foretaste of the church that holds to Christ. Jesus took the phrase abomination of desolation onto His own lips, tying this chapter to His coming and the end.
basic.god: God is sovereign over history in breathtaking detail, foretelling kings, marriages, betrayals, and battles centuries ahead. He sets the appointed times, permits persecution for a bounded season, uses suffering to refine His people, and brings every arrogant tyrant to his end exactly when He has decreed. Nothing escapes His command.
basic.crossReferences: First and second Maccabees record the persecution under Antiochus. Matthew 24 has Jesus cite the abomination of desolation. And 2 Thessalonians 2 describes the man of lawlessness who exalts himself, destroyed by Christ.
basic.oneStory: History's wars and tyrants are laid out in advance to show that God holds every thread. A blasphemous king will defile what is holy and crush the faithful, but only for an appointed time, and then he will fall with no one to help him. The pattern points to the final enemy and his certain defeat by Christ, and to the wise who, through suffering, hold fast to the God who governs it all.

context.literary: The detailed core of the final vision, framed by the heavenly encounter of chapter 10 and the resurrection promise of chapter 12. It moves as a rapid survey of kings without naming them, using the recurring titles king of the South and king of the North, and rising to a climax in the self-exalting king and his fall.
context.historical: The prophecy corresponds closely to the Hellenistic era: Alexander the Great, the division of his empire, and the long conflict between the Ptolemies of Egypt (the South) and the Seleucids of Syria (the North), climaxing in Antiochus IV Epiphanes, who around 167 BC desecrated the temple, banned the sacrifice, and provoked the Maccabean resistance. The precision has led some critics to date Daniel late; the book itself presents it as genuine prophecy given to Daniel in exile, and Christians receive it as such, with humility about the details.
context.canonical: The chapter's imagery of a covenant-defiling, self-exalting king feeds directly into the New Testament portrait of the antichrist and the man of lawlessness, and the abomination of desolation is quoted by Jesus. The theme of the refined and suffering wise connects to the beatitudes and to the New Testament theology of suffering that purifies faith.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To demonstrate God's absolute sovereignty over the detailed course of history, to forewarn and fortify God's people for a coming persecution, to call the wise to faithful resistance and instruction whatever the cost, and to assure them that the tyrant's time is fixed and his end certain.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter predicts and describes the deeds of kings; it is not prescribing their behavior or handing readers a code for dating the end. Its enduring calls are to know God and stand firm, to instruct many, to accept refining through suffering, and to trust God's appointed times.

debates.question: Do verses 36 to 45 still describe Antiochus IV, or a future antichrist?

views.label: Still Antiochus IV, with some details generalized
views.case: The self-exalting king continues the figure of verses 21 to 35, and much of the description fits Antiochus, who blasphemed, defiled the temple, and honored a war god; on this view the chapter stays within the Hellenistic crisis, though some final details are compressed or stylized.

views.label: A shift to the final antichrist
views.case: The language of the time of the end, the king magnifying himself above every god and speaking against the God of gods, and details that do not neatly fit Antiochus, lead many to see a transition here from Antiochus to a greater end-time enemy, whom Antiochus foreshadows and whom Christ will destroy, echoing 2 Thessalonians 2.

debates.bearing: Whether read of Antiochus, a future antichrist, or Antiochus as a type of that final enemy, the assurance is the same: the blasphemous tyrant will reach his appointed end with no one to help him, and God's people will be vindicated.

study.originalLanguages: The whole conflict is a war over the berith, the holy covenant: the tyrant sets his heart against it, defiles the sanctuary, and rewards those who forsake it, setting up the shiqquts, the abomination that lays the temple waste. But he does not have the last word among God's people, for the maskilim, the wise who know their God, stand firm, instruct many, and are refined through their suffering. And over the whole chapter hangs the word moed, the appointed time: the persecution, the resistance, and the tyrant's fall all come precisely when God has fixed, so that even in the darkest hour His people know the clock is His.

typology.type: The abomination of desolation in the sanctuary
typology.fulfillment: The pattern Jesus applies to coming judgment and the ultimate defiler defeated by Christ, Matthew 24:15 and 2 Thessalonians 2:4.

typology.type: The self-exalting king who blasphemes the God of gods
typology.fulfillment: The man of lawlessness and antichrist, destroyed by the brightness of Christ's coming, 2 Thessalonians 2:8.

typology.type: The wise who suffer, teach, and are refined
typology.fulfillment: The witnesses and martyrs who share Christ's sufferings and are purified, 1 Peter 1:6-7 and Revelation 12:11.

study.christ: The king who exalts himself above every god, blasphemes the God of gods, and defiles the sanctuary is the very portrait of the antichrist, the man of lawlessness whom the Lord Jesus will slay with the breath of His mouth. Jesus Himself took the phrase abomination of desolation from this vision and applied it to the judgment to come, binding this chapter to His own return. And against every such tyrant stand the people who know their God and hold firm, a foretaste of the church that clings to Christ through tribulation. The suffering of the wise, refined and made spotless, is taken up into the sufferings of Christ, in whom the faithful are purified and finally vindicated.
study.god: God is sovereign over history in astonishing detail, foretelling the rise and fall of kings, their marriages, treacheries, and campaigns, centuries before they occur. He appoints the times, sets bounds to the worst persecution, and even uses the suffering of His people to refine and purify their faith. He permits the blasphemer to prosper only until the time of wrath is completed, for what He has decreed must be accomplished, and then the proud king falls with no one to help him. History is His, down to its smallest turn.

crossReferences.note: The same insolent king who defies the Prince of princes and is broken by no human hand.
Jesus cites the abomination of desolation spoken of by Daniel, applying the pattern to future judgment.
The man of lawlessness who exalts himself above every god and is destroyed by Christ's coming.
Trials that refine faith like gold, illuminating the refining of the wise who fall in Daniel 11:35.

study.oneStory: The tangled wars of kings are laid out in advance to prove that God holds every thread of history in His hand. A blasphemous tyrant will defile what is holy and crush the faithful, but only until the appointed time, and then he will fall with no one to help him. The pattern reaches forward to the final enemy and his certain defeat by Christ, and to the wise who, refined through suffering, hold fast to the God who governs all things and who will surely vindicate His people at the time He has set.

quiz.q: What does the mighty king whose empire is broken to the four winds represent?

options.text: The Greek king Alexander, whose vast empire was divided after him and did not pass to his descendants.
options.note: Daniel 11:3-4 matches Alexander and the division of his empire.

options.text: A future Roman emperor.
options.note: The details fit the Greek king Alexander, not a Roman emperor.

options.text: King Nebuchadnezzar of Babylon.
options.note: Babylon has already fallen; this is the Greek kingdom.

options.text: King David of Israel.
options.note: This concerns a Gentile empire divided to the four winds, not David.

quiz.q: What was the great offense of the despicable king against God's people?

options.text: He set himself against the holy covenant, halted the daily sacrifice, and set up the abomination of desolation.
options.note: Daniel 11:28-31 centers his crime on defiling the covenant and temple.

options.text: He merely raised taxes on the people.
options.note: Taxation appears earlier of another king; his defining sin was defiling the covenant and sanctuary.

options.text: He rebuilt the temple grander than before.
options.note: The opposite; he desecrated the temple and stopped the sacrifice.

options.text: He freed the Jewish people from oppression.
options.note: He persecuted them and defiled their worship.

quiz.q: How do the people who know their God respond in the persecution?

options.text: They firmly resist him, and the wise instruct many, though some fall and are refined through suffering.
options.note: Daniel 11:32-35 shows faithful resistance and refining.

options.text: They all abandon their faith to survive.
options.note: Some join insincerely, but the people who know God stand firm.

options.text: They flee and are never heard from again.
options.note: They resist and teach many, even at the cost of their lives.

options.text: They join the king's cause enthusiastically.
options.note: They resist him firmly; it is the covenant violators he corrupts.

quiz.q: What is the repeated assurance about the timing of these events?

options.text: Everything comes at the appointed time God has fixed, and the end will still come as decreed.
options.note: Daniel 11:27, 29, 35 anchor events to God's appointed time.

options.text: The events happen randomly and cannot be predicted.
options.note: The opposite; the vision stresses God's fixed, appointed times.

options.text: The tyrant sets the timing himself.
options.note: The tyrant prospers only until the time of wrath God has decreed is complete.

options.text: No one, not even God, knows when the end will come.
options.note: The end comes at God's appointed time, already decreed.

quiz.q: How does the self-exalting king point to Christ's victory?

options.text: He is a type of the antichrist who blasphemes God, yet meets his end with no one to help, foreshadowing the final enemy Christ destroys.
options.note: The pattern points to the man of lawlessness destroyed by Christ, 2 Thessalonians 2:8.

options.text: He shows that arrogant kings always win in the end.
options.note: The opposite; he comes to his end with no one to help him.

options.text: He teaches that God cannot stop powerful rulers.
options.note: God brings him to his appointed end; He is fully sovereign.

options.text: He has no connection to Christ.
options.note: The blasphemous king prefigures the antichrist whom Christ defeats.

questions: God foretold the wars of kings in astonishing detail. How does His command of history's smallest turns give you confidence in the middle of chaotic world events?
The people who knew their God stood firm when others compromised. What does it look like for you to know God deeply enough to resist pressure?
The wise instructed many even while suffering. Who has God placed around you to teach and strengthen, even at personal cost?
Some of the wise fell in order to be refined and made spotless. How does it reframe your trials to see them as God refining your faith rather than abandoning you?
The proud king prospered only until the appointed time, then fell. How does the certainty that evil has a fixed limit help you endure a season when the wicked seem to win?

translationNotes.note: Greece renders the Hebrew Javan; the mighty king who follows is widely identified with Alexander the Great.
Ships of Kittim refers to western coastlands or Mediterranean islands, historically the intervention of Rome that turned Antiochus back from Egypt.
The abomination of desolation is the phrase Jesus takes up in Matthew 24:15; here it marks the desecration of the temple and halting of the daily sacrifice.
The one desired by women is variously understood; the point is the king's utter contempt for every god and loyalty, magnifying only himself.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Daniel 11:2-45. Em dashes in verses 24 and 38 converted to commas. Chapter 11 is Hebrew, so load-bearing lexicon entries use Hebrew. No em or en dashes used.
